text: --- PAGE 1 --- EXHIBIT C Case 1:22-cr-00673-LAK Document 407-32 Filed 02/27/24 Page 1 of 5 --- PAGE 2 --- The Wayback Machine Ԁhttps ԟ//web ԛarchive ԛorg/web/ ӅҿӅҿӃҿӅӗӃӅӃӓҿҿ/https ԟ//blog ԛftx ԛcom/blog/our Ԁliquidation Ԁengine/ Our Liquidation Engine Ԇ how we significantly reduced the likelihood of clawbacks from ever occurring Note ԟsince this article was published ԙOKEx has significantly revamped their liquidation engine ԙbringing it more in line with industry norms ԛAs of May ӅҿӅҿԙFTX has in fact never had a clawback ԛ It’s first worth making explicit ԟthe thing that causes socialized losses ԙand clawbacks ԙand auto Ԁdelevering ԙis when an account goes beyond bankrupt ԛIf a user has a leveraged futures position on and markets move against their account enough that their net asset value is negative ԙthen someone has to pay for that loss Ԣ and in crypto you can’t reposses assets from the bankrupt account’s owner from outside the system ԙso you’re stuck with other users Ԇ the users who aren’t getting liquidated Ԇ footing the bill ԛ Like most liquidation engines ԙthe one FTX uses starts by detecting when a user has dropped below maintenance margin ԛ Unlike many other platforms it chooses intelligent ԙefficient values for these Ԇ some other platforms ԙlike OKEx ԙare fucked by the time a liquidation starts because their maintenance margin was too low and there is no way for them to liquidate such a large position so quickly ԛ We send reasonable ԙvolume Ԁlimited liquidation orders to close down positions that drop below maintenance margin Өwhich starts at ӌԛӎ% and increases with position size ӪԛWe don’t sell so quickly that the liquidation orders themselves will crash the market Ԣthat would be dooming the entire process ԛWe also don’t > $c d/ f t x şresearch Also available in Blog Change Log Market Recap Monthly Digest Videos Case 1:22-cr-00673-LAK Document 407-32 Filed 02/27/24 Page 2 of 5 --- PAGE 3 --- give up if the price looks ‘bad’ Ԇ it might only get worse from there and you have to do the best you can liquidating an account rather than hoping things magically reverse Өas OKEx does Ӫԛ Usually this is enough ԛ But if there’s a large liquidation Ԇ say a long position Өposition B Ӫ that needs to get sold off Ԇ and markets are moving down too quickly ԙ it might become clear that the normal liquidation orders in FTX’s orderbooks are unlikely to successfully close down position B before the account goes bankrupt ԛ In that case ԙ the backstop liquidity provider system kicks in ԛ In this situation ԙ liquidity providers who have opted in to the system will internalize position B ԙ taking over the whole obligation and collateral ԛ They’ll do this before the account actually goes bankrupt so they have a chance to successfully manage the position ԛ They will then go hedge their books on other venues ԛ This effectively allows the backstop liquidity providers to instantly inject liquidity from other exchanges into FTX in an emergency ԙ removing the dangerous account’s position from FTX’s books and preventing a likely bankrupcy ԛ It’s worth noting here the difference between the role that an insurance fund plays from the role that a liquidity provider plays ԛ If someone has a long Ӆҿҿm position Ԇ and so if markets keep moving the insurance fund will just keep losing more and more and more ԛ The way to actually end a liquidation is for someone to take that position of the client’s hands Ԇ that is someone to actually take on the long $Ӆҿҿm of BTC exposure ԛ In this way the backstop liquidity provider program solves a problem that no insurance fund can solve ԙ no matter how large ԛ Hopefully ԙ the backstop liquidity provider program will be enough to prevent any clawbacks from occurring ԛ In our testing ԙ even market moves of ӌҿ% in a Ӆҿ minute period were not enough to cause clawbacks Ԣ the combination of on Ԁexchange liquidity and backstop liquidity providers were able to provide to all of the nearly bankrupt accounts before they went under ԛ In fact the insurance fund actually gained about $ Ӄ m in most of these scenarios ԛ But there needs to be a worst case scenario ԛ And if all else fails ԙ FTX will do what other exchanges do Ԇ it will auto Ԁdelever an account’s position against accounts that have the opposite position on ԙ and attempt to cover any losses out of the insurance fund Ԣ and if the insurance fund runs dry then there will be clawbacks ԛ But FTX really does see clawbacks as a worst case scenario that we hope never happens ԛ We designed a system that we think will withstand huge market moves

in most of these scenarios ԛ But there needs to be a worst case scenario ԛ And if all else fails ԙ FTX will do what other exchanges do Ԇ it will auto Ԁdelever an account’s position against accounts that have the opposite position on ԙ and attempt to cover any losses out of the insurance fund Ԣ and if the insurance fund runs dry then there will be clawbacks ԛ But FTX really does see clawbacks as a worst case scenario that we hope never happens ԛ We designed a system that we think will withstand huge market moves and huge volume without leading to any clawbacks ԛ And if there’s ever a clawback on FTX ԙ we fucked up ԛ We will apologize ԙ write a detailed post explaining the mistakes we made to get the market to the point that clawbacks were necessary ԙ and issue IOUs against future insurance fund increases for any shortfall ԛ Because that’s what should happen if an exchange loses customers’ money ԙ even if it’s far from the norm right now ԛ /g13/g11/g11/g29/g4/g16  Case 1:22-cr-00673-LAK Document 407-32 Filed 02/27/24 Page 3 of 5 --- PAGE 4 --- Anyway Ԇ that’s the FTX manifesto on liquidations ԛ How are our competitors doing? We’d like to start by explicitly saying that all things considered BitMEX has done a good job at preventing clawbacks ԛ There are occasional auto Ԁdeleveragings and socialized losses on the illiquid altcoin contracts but they’re rare and the BTC perpetual swaps Ԇ the one really liquid product on BitMEX Ԇ virtually never sees them ԛ But OKEx is in a pretty bad state ԛ There was a well Ԁpublicized $ӛm clawback on July ӈӃst ӅҿӃӗԛ Quoting from OKEx’s official blog post on the incident ԟ Ӄԛ OKEx saw this as business as usual ԟ “OKEx has adopted the societal loss risk management mechanism since launched and it has been working orderly as intended ԛ Ӆԛ OKEx’s attempt to address the situation ԟ “Our risk management team immediately contacted the client ԙ requesting the client several times to partially close the positions to reduce the overall market risks ԛ However ԙ the client refused to cooperate… ” ӈԛ If OKEx was not going to be able to handle the order why did they let the client place it? OKEx’s risk systems are woefully inadequate ԛ ӌԛ “Shortly after this preemptive action ԙ unfortunately ԙ the BTC price tumbled ԙ causing the liquidation of the account ԛ” ӎԛ OKEx seems not to be fully aware of why the BTC price tumbled ԛ “Unfortunate” is one way of putting it Ԣ “in response to OKEx manually placing a $ ӌҿҿm liquidation offer and hoping against hope someone would trade against it Ԇ but instead seeing the market update on the expected impact of the order” is another ԛ OKEx caused the move that caused the clawback ԛ ӓԛ “We will implement a series of risk management enhancements ԙ which are in line with our futures roadmap released on July Ӄӕԙ ӅҿӃӗԙ to prevent any similar cases from occurring again ԛ” So how has this new framework done? ӕԛ On ӅҿӃӗԂӃӃԂӅӈ OKEx clawed back ӎҿ% of profit from ETC futures ԙ or over $ Ӄmԛ ӗԛ On ӅҿӃӗԂҿӛԂҿӕԙ OKEx clawed back Ӄӗ% of profit from EOS futures ԙ or over $ ӈmԛ ӛԛ OKEx’s BSV futures went through a ӓ week stretch from ӅҿӃӗԂ ӃӅԂӅӗ to ӅҿӃӛԂҿӅԂҿӃ where the average clawback was ӌӅ% ԛ Ӄҿԛ The now Ԁdelisted OKEx BTG futures went through periods of having over ӗҿ% clawbacks each week Ԇ meaning that nearly the entire trading in the futures was someone going bankrupt! ӃӃԛ And ԙ infamously ԙ a few days before the BCH fork OKEx emergency settled their futures early Ԇ without notice Ԇ to a marked to market price in a downlimit future trading against the wrong index ԙ leading to roughly $ Ӆҿm of losses ԛ ӃӅԛ Huobi’s product ԙ HBDM ԙ has not yet had clawbacks Ԇ to their credit ԛ But their product is only a month old Ԣ and given that the product was copy Ԁpasted from OKEx’s ԙ it should not be expected to withstand the test of time ԛ You can see the specs on FTX ԛ You can also see a flowchart of the liquidation process in our previous article ԛ Note ԟ since this article was published ԙ OKEx has significantly revamped their liquidation engine ԙ bringing it more in line with Case 1:22-cr-00673-LAK Document 407-32 Filed 02/27/24 Page 4 of 5 --- PAGE 5 --- industry norms by borrowing elements seen in lots of other platforms ԙ including FTX ԛ As of May ӅҿӅҿԙ FTX has in fact never had a clawback ԛ Sam Bankman ԀFried Case 1:22-cr-00673-LAK Document
